title: Make an Emergency Contact Card
summary: Create a small card with emergency contacts and medical info to carry or display.
learnerPromise: You will be able to create a portable card with your key emergency contacts and basic medical information.
whyItMatters: In an urgent situation, having contact and health details ready can help others assist you faster, especially if you cannot speak for yourself.
realLifeUses: Carrying it in your wallet or bag so it is always with you
Taping it inside a locker or on the fridge at home
Giving a copy to a trusted friend, roommate, or caregiver
Sharing it with a teacher, coach, or supervisor who may need to help you
materialsNeeded: Index card, paper, or small notebook page
Optional: clear tape or laminating sleeve to protect the card
result: A finished emergency contact card with at least three contacts and one piece of health information.
youWillLearn: Decide which contacts and health details are most important to include
Format information so it is easy to read in a stressful moment
Choose a safe place to keep the card where others can find it
Update the card when information changes
miniChallenge: Write down three emergency contacts and one medical detail on a card. Place it somewhere you or others can easily find it.
steps: Write your full name at the top of the card.
List three trusted contacts with their relationship to you and phone numbers.
Add one relevant health detail, such as a medicine allergy or condition.
Write down the local emergency number in a bold or highlighted spot.
Decide where to keep the card and put it there now.
completionCriteria: Your card has at least three contacts and one health or allergy note
You have placed the card in a location where you or someone helping you can find it quickly
commonProblems: Phone numbers change and the card becomes outdated
The card gets damaged if not protected from water or wear
It can be hard to decide which contacts to include if you have a large support network
tips: Include both a local contact and one out-of-area contact in case local lines are down
Use a waterproof sleeve or tape to protect the card if you carry it daily
Review and update the card every six months or after any big life change
helpBoundary: Ask a trusted adult, caregiver, or support person for help if you are unsure which medical details to include or do not have anyone you feel comfortable listing as a contact.
